insight: The DD/DDE opening (third engineer as P1) is only justified when there is a big absorber in the set (larger than a 3HP Wall), or when a costly but powerful unit like Omega Splitter or Lucina is present. Without such a justification, P1 should generally develop a tech building (Conduit, Animus, or Blastforge) on turn 3 instead.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about P1 opening strategies and when DD/DDE is appropriate versus suboptimal.

insight: The general economic priority in Prismata is: absorb economy > attack economy > drone economy. Getting attack economy (tech buildings for attackers) early is usually preferable to simply buying more drones, unless there is a strong absorber in the set to justify a high-drone economy.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about P1 opening choices and general economy priorities.

insight: In a Breachproof game plan, the general micro priority is to buy Trinity Drones before attackers, with rare exceptions for supply considerations. Targeting the fabricator (Gauss Fab) at low lifespan (e.g., 3 duration) is typically a mistake; a 5-duration Gauss Fab is where targeting it becomes worthwhile, and 4-duration is roughly break-even at best.
author: extratricky
context: Post-game analysis of a Breachproof game where the losing player mismanaged targeting priority.

insight: Shadowfang is approximately the second most efficient attacker in the game (after Lucina), but its high triple-red tech cost makes it impractical unless the set has red fixing (Mobile Animus, Flame Animus, Chrono Filter) or a good gold sink for the fourth red. Shadowfang scales better than Cynestra when you plan to mass it, since having the tech for one means you can afford one every turn.
author: consensus
context: New player asked when Shadowfang is preferable to Cynestra; multiple experts explained the conditions.

insight: Militia is primarily strong in two specific contexts: Breachproof setups, and Iso Kronus / synchronized attacker strategies. As a first attacker it is acceptable (denies absorb if opponent runs out), but massing Militia is a losing strategy. It should not be 'rushed' in the sense of being the centerpiece of an aggressive build.
author: swapgo
context: New player asked whether Militia was underpowered; experts clarified its niche uses.

insight: Savior should not be built in the first few turns because it costs 6 drones in addition to its gold cost, severely crippling your economy due to compound interest effects. The default Savior timing for P1 is around turn 4 (opening: DD/DDE/DDD/DD1), and slightly later for P2 (DD/DDE/DDD/DDDE/1). The best support units for Savior are cheap soak like Polywall (buyable with one or two Blastforges), since Savior needs something to survive while it is being constructed.
author: apooche
context: New player asked about optimal Savior timing; apooche gave specific turn numbers and amalloy/liadahlia elaborated on supporting units.

insight: Iceblade Golems are countered by either reaching exactly 6 attack (which wrecks their attack efficiency and denies absorb) or going Breachproof (which makes them useless). Iceblades are particularly good against blockers with even health values like Infusion Grid or Doomed Walls, and also counter Plexo Cell.
author: swapgo
context: Player asked how to counter Iceblade Golems after being overwhelmed by them.

insight: The (then-recent) buff to Thermite Core giving it 2 Pixies was considered too strong by multiple community members. The buff allowed denial of any and all absorb up to 9 attack and crucially did not hurt its versatility, since an odd amount of attack can be handled by clicking one Pixie before clicking Thermite. The only situation where versatility is reduced is when a player has exactly 1 attack and no other attackers built.
author: namington
context: Discussion about whether the recent Thermite Core buff was overpowered; namington, liadahlia, and others agreed it was strong.

insight: Redeemer should generally be treated like a regular attacker, with the key exception that the turn its Gauss Charges arrive creates a burst vulnerability window. Redeemer is stronger when good soak (e.g., Aegis) is available to absorb the charges, and weaker when the opponent has burst damage (e.g., Nivo Charge) that can exploit that window. When facing a Redeemer player, maintaining your own damage output prevents them from simply absorbing the charge turns.
author: 307th
context: Player asked for tips on playing with and against Redeemer after consistently losing to it.

insight: In Thorium Dynamo sets, the correct opening in the vast majority of cases is DD/DTh (buying Thorium Dynamo on turn 2 alongside a Drone), as stated by an expert player.
author: masn6811
context: Brief comment affirming the dominant opening for Thorium Dynamo sets.

insight: Absorb denial is a powerful mechanic because the value of each attack point is near-zero before it crosses the opponent's absorb threshold, meaning units like Bloodrager and Lancetooth that deny absorb are effective even when their raw stats look unimpressive. This is also why the attack economy is generally better than the drone economy.
author: liadahlia
context: Discussion about the value of attack relative to absorb thresholds and why absorb-denial units are strong.

insight: Arka Sodara is unusual among big units in that fast attack actually supports it rather than being detrimental: reaching 7 attack quickly makes getting Arka much easier, whereas most large units require good defense to function because fast opponent attack threatens them before they come online.
author: liadahlia
context: Discussion about whether Arka Sodara is good on low econ or in BSO+1 sets.

insight: The opening DD/DD/DDEC (getting both an Engineer and Conduit on turn 3 as P1) is nearly always worse than DD/DDE/DDD/DDDC. The only conceivable justification for DD/DD/DDEC is a rock-paper-scissors situation where delaying tech commitment has unusual value, but passing on a drone on turn 3 is very costly.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about the 3rd Engineer Conduit opening and when DDEC vs DDDC is correct.

insight: In a set with Xeno Guardian and Wincer alongside Hellhounds and Cryo Rays, the correct plan is to sequence: get one Xeno Guardian to absorb on, then a Wincer, then spam Hellhounds and Cryo Rays. Building an early Animus without following up with attackers quickly (e.g., getting a second Animus before any Blastforge) wastes the tech investment.
author: apooche
context: Post-game analysis for a new player whose opening misallocated tech resources in a Hellhound/Cryo Ray set.

insight: 'Soak' refers to defenders that are purchased with the explicit intent of sacrificing them on defense rather than preserving them, distinct from 'absorbers' which are durable defenders meant to survive repeated attacks.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy defined the term 'soak' for a new player during a Savior strategy discussion.
